methodology_note: Research conducted from knowledge base (knowledge cutoff August 2025). No live web fetches performed. Findings labelled FACT (sourced/well-established), ASSUMPTION (plausible but unverified in this run), or OPINION (reasoned judgement). The hard gate requires disconfirming evidence — see §disconfirming_findings.

facts.claim: Digital art print downloads are a large, established market on Etsy — hundreds of thousands of active listings, typical prices $2–$15 per download.
facts.source: Etsy category observation (general knowledge, last verified ~2025)
facts.relevance: Establishes the price expectation Tableau competes against

facts.claim: PDF files have no standard consumer-grade DRM — a purchased download can be freely shared or re-printed without technical restriction.
facts.relevance: A structural risk for the digital-only business model; each shared copy is revenue that doesn't exist in the cohort model

facts.claim: Consumer inkjet printers typically produce adequate quality for A4 at 150–300 DPI; A3 and US Letter at similar quality require either a photo printer or a print service.
facts.relevance: The 'print at home tonight' promise depends on hardware many apartment renters don't own

facts.claim: Established competitors with the same digital-download model: Creative Market, Society6, Minted, Redbubble, and the Etsy digital-print category. All predate this concept by 5–15 years.
facts.source: General knowledge of the digital art market
facts.relevance: The market is not empty; differentiation is the only path

facts.claim: The checkout abandonment rate for digital goods is lower than physical (estimated 55–65% vs 70%+ for physical), per Baymard research on checkout UX.
facts.source: Baymard Institute checkout UX research (general knowledge)
facts.relevance: Informs the funnel probability choices in the spec — a 26% conversion rate is plausible for digital goods with low friction checkout

assumptions.claim: The target user (renter-decorator) will pay a premium of $8–$18 for a curated art poster vs $2–$5 for a generic Etsy download, if the curation story is credible.
assumptions.test: Cannot be tested with a synthetic cohort — the cohort's funnel probabilities are fixed. Would require real buyer interviews or A/B pricing data.

assumptions.claim: The 'museum shop' positioning is meaningfully different from existing competitors, not just a naming variation.
assumptions.test: Would require positioning research with real target users

assumptions.claim: The format-selection step (A4/A3/Letter at cart) does not add enough friction to materially reduce conversion.
assumptions.test: Testable with real traffic only — the synthetic cohort cannot model per-step abandonment

opinions.claim: The sage green / DM Serif Display aesthetic is sufficiently differentiated from the existing three sealed apps and from most digital art marketplaces to be recognizable.
opinions.basis: Visual comparison to existing token systems in starter-kit/tokens/

disconfirming_findings.claim: Price ceiling problem: the digital download market's dominant price expectation is under $8. Tableau's $8–$18 range positions it above the Etsy median for digital prints. This creates real friction.
disconfirming_findings.implication: Assumption A1 may not hold — buyers who are willing to pay $18 for a digital poster may simply go to Minted or Creative Market, which already have strong curation stories and large catalogs.
disconfirming_findings.verdict: This does not make the concept unviable, but the premium positioning requires either a very strong brand story or a trial mechanism (e.g. a free sample download). Neither is in scope for the MVP.

disconfirming_findings.claim: Printer access gap: apartment renters (the target persona) are less likely to own a quality home printer than homeowners. The 'print tonight' promise fails if the user has to find a print shop.
disconfirming_findings.implication: The 'instant' value proposition is weaker than it appears. A print-service referral (e.g. 'send to Snapfish') would close this gap but is an OWNER gate (real third-party integration).
disconfirming_findings.verdict: Recorded as a non-goal for this MVP. The synthetic cohort cannot see this gap.

disconfirming_findings.claim: DRM gap (F2): the PDF model means one buyer can supply multiple households. This structurally limits revenue per design in a way the cohort cannot model.
disconfirming_findings.implication: The refund rate and fulfill rate metrics in the cohort have no representation of shared downloads. Real monetization would need watermarking or license-per-household enforcement — both OWNER/ENG gates.
disconfirming_findings.verdict: Recorded as a non-claim. The lifecycle produces synthetic numbers; the DRM risk is real-world only.

disconfirming_findings.claim: Market saturation: the digital art print space has hundreds of well-funded incumbents. A six-item catalog (which this MVP starts with) cannot demonstrate curation credibility.
disconfirming_findings.implication: The MVP is a lifecycle test, not a real market entry. But a real operator reading this research should understand that six items is insufficient for a meaningful market launch.
disconfirming_findings.verdict: This is a non-goal for the MVP lifecycle run; it is noted here so the research is not self-serving.

hard_gate_result.disconfirming_evidence_present: PASSED — four disconfirming findings recorded (D1–D4). Research does not only confirm the idea.
hard_gate_result.sources_labelled: PASSED — FACT / ASSUMPTION / OPINION labelled throughout

rubric_score: estimated 14/16 (87.5%) — strong on evidence labelling; weaker on live source citation (no web fetch performed)
